glossaryTerms.definition: a highly deflocculated refined-clay slip that, when burnished, gives a low sheen surviving low-fire temperatures
the drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, carve, and join
fully air-dried but unfired clay: fragile and at maximum absorbency
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

Terra sigillata is a very fine clay slip. It is made by deflocculating clay in water and letting the coarse particles settle out. Only the finest particles remain. These fine particles give the slip its burnishing quality.
Apply it to
leather-hard
or near-
bone-dry

stoneware, then burnish with a smooth stone or the back of a spoon. The particles compact to give a low satin sheen. This sheen survives earthenware temperatures (cone 06 to cone 04, about 1000 to 1060 degrees C). At stoneware temperatures (cone 6, about 1220 degrees C), the body vitrifies and the sheen partly fades.
Terra sigillata
works best as a single-fire earthenware surface, or on stoneware where a soft sheen is the aim.
When to use it

Apply terra sigillata to the exterior of stoneware pieces where a matte-to-satin surface is preferred over a standard glaze. It works on any smooth stoneware piece at leather-hard or very firm stages. On pieces fired at cone 06 to cone 04, it is a full replacement for glaze. The surface looks sealed without a conventional glaze layer. At cone 6, the fired surface stays slightly porous. It is not food-safe without an extra glaze layer. At full stoneware temperatures, terra sigillata is normally used on decorative exterior surfaces only. Apply a food-safe glaze to the interior.
What you need

materials.name: Smooth stoneware piece (leather-hard to near-bone-dry)
materials.quantity: one piece at correct drying stage

materials.name: Terra sigillata (red)
materials.quantity: small bottle or jar, enough for 2-3 coats

content.text: Step by step

Confirm the piece is at leather-hard to near-bone-dry. Very firm leather-hard is the best stage. The surface should be cool to the touch but no longer damp. Terra sigillata on very wet leather-hard clay sits on the surface and does not bond well. On fully bone-dry clay, it can still be applied, but burnishing risks scratching the brittle surface.
bone-dry
clay, the application is still possible but burnishing risks scratching the brittle surface.

Gently swirl the terra sigillata bottle before opening. Do not shake it vigorously: the fine-particle suspension is stable but shaking introduces air bubbles that dry as pinholes on the surface. Pour a small amount into a clean shallow dish.

Apply the first coat with the hake brush in long, even strokes in one direction. The coat should be thin and even: terra sigillata is applied in very thin layers, thinner than conventional slip. The surface should take on a slight sheen within a minute as the water evaporates from the thin film.
Allow the first coat to dry until the sheen disappears and the surface is dry to the touch. Apply a second coat in the opposite direction, crossing the first coat at 90 degrees. Allow to dry again.
Apply a third coat if desired for a deeper colour. Two to three coats is standard. More than three coats risks flaking at the surface boundary.

Burnish the surface after the final coat has dried to touch but while it is still slightly cool to the touch. Use the back of a smooth spoon, a polished river stone, or your fingertip. Rub in overlapping circles with light but consistent pressure. The fine clay particles align under the burnishing tool and the surface develops a low satin sheen. Do not press so hard that you scratch through to the clay body below.
Fire the piece to
cone

06 (approximately 1000 degrees C) for maximum sheen preservation. At cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C), expect the sheen to reduce to a subdued matte-satin rather than the full burnished glow. If firing to cone 6, glaze the interior with a food-safe glaze as the terra sigillata exterior at full stoneware temperature is not food-safe.
What it looks like when it works

Before firing, the burnished surface has a quiet, even satin sheen. It is not glassy and not completely matte. The light falls differently across the burnishing strokes, giving a subtle directional quality. After firing at cone 06, the surface keeps this quality in a slightly more subdued form. There is a warm tone from the iron-rich red terra sigillata. After firing at cone 6, the surface is uniform red-brown and matte-to-satin. It is pleasant and distinctive, but without the high burnish of a low-fire result.
Common mistakes

Applying too thickly: a thick coat of terra sigillata will flake off the surface during drying or firing, leaving a patchy result. Each coat should be thin enough to dry within two minutes on the clay surface.
Burnishing too early (slip still wet): the tool smears the wet slip rather than aligning the particles. The surface needs to be dry to touch before burnishing.
Expecting a full gloss at cone 6: at stoneware temperatures the burnish is partly lost. Use terra sigillata for a matte-satin earthenware result, or on stoneware where a subdued sheen is fine.

Applying to bone-dry clay without care: bone-dry clay is absorbent and fragile; the terra sigillata applies successfully but the burnishing tool can scratch the surface if pressed too firmly. Work with very light pressure.
